summary: Gruber uses the disappearance of document proxy icons in macOS Big Sur as a case study for Apple's broader UI philosophy of hiding interface elements until needed. He argues that proxy icons aren't clutter but useful, discoverable features that reinforce the Mac's foundational concept of showing users what they can do. He traces the Mac's original breakthrough as making actions visible and discoverable, contrasting it with both the minimalism of a terminal and Microsoft's opposite extreme of toolbar overload. He concludes that UI design is a Goldilocks problem where Apple may be swinging too far toward hiding things.
key_points: Apple's 'you should only see a button when you need it' philosophy drove the removal of proxy icons in macOS Big Sur, treating them as clutter
Proxy icons are genuinely useful and keeping them visible made them discoverable — hiding them undermines the Mac's core principle of visual exploration
The Mac's original breakthrough was showing users what they could do, not just what they had done — proxy icons exemplified this philosophy
UI minimalism taken too far approaches the terminal's blinking cursor, which is the opposite of what made the Mac successful
Deciding what to expose at the top level is a Goldilocks problem — Microsoft went too far with toolbar icons, but Apple risks going too far the other way
money_quotes: In a sense, no personal computer interface can out-minimalize an old terminal command line — just a blinking cursor on a black screen, awaiting your commands.
The Mac's breakthrough was establishing an interface where you could see — and thus discover through visual exploration — not just what you had done, but what you could do.
They said: Even though this document is open in an editing window, you can still do things with the file — here it is.
I'd argue strenuously that proxy icons aren't needless clutter — they're useful, and showing them by default made them discoverable.
Keeping them visible reminds you that they're there.
It's devilishly hard work deciding what to expose at the top level of a user interface.
Like almost every design challenge, it's a Goldilocks problem — you can go too far in the other direction, and there is no 'just right' that will please everyone.
key_insight: Apple's drive toward UI minimalism risks undermining the Mac's foundational principle that the best interfaces show users what they can do, not just what they've done.
